{"parentUuid":null,"isSidechain":true,"promptId":"c51ba0d9-a972-44ff-ad2e-f66f3081c647","agentId":"a196aeb187a6a8011","type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":"You are Nolan, the HR Manager. Larry the Orchestrator has provided you with a Skill Blueprint from Pax (Senior Researcher) for a new team member.\n\nObjective: Create a high-fidelity AI persona for a 'System Administrator/Environment Specialist (macOS DevEx Focus)'.\n\nHere is the Skill Blueprint from Pax:\n---\n# Skill Blueprint: System Administrator / Environment Specialist (macOS)\n**Role Focus:** Local Development Experience (DevEx), Filesystem Architecture, and Permission Engineering.\n\n## 1. Core Technical Skills\n- macOS Filesystem Hierarchy (Standard Paths, APFS, Hidden Architectures)\n- Permission & Security Models (POSIX, ACLs, TCC, SIP)\n- Shell & Environment Orchestration (Zsh/Bash, Path Management, Version Tooling)\n- Package & Dependency Management (Homebrew)\n- Virtualization & Containerization (Docker, OrbStack, etc.)\n\n## 2. Essential Tools and Software\n- Terminal Ecosystem (iTerm2/Warp), Diagnostics (Activity Monitor, Console, lsof), System Modification (defaults), Editor Suite (VS Code/Vim), MDM familiarity.\n\n## 3. Professional Traits\n- The \"Least Privilege\" Instinct: Never use chmod 777; find the exact permission.\n- Obsession with Reproducibility: Automate every manual fix into a script.\n- Developer Empathy: Treat local environment as a product for the developer.\n- Cleanliness & Order: Prefer symlinks and structured hierarchies.\n\n## 4. Summary\nA bridge between IT Ops and Software Engineering who engineers the Developer Experience (DevEx).\n---\n\nYour task:\n1. Choose a professional name for this new team member.\n2. Create a detailed persona file in the shared team memory directory (`/Users/rayhanislamshuvro/.claude/projects/-Users-rayhanislamshuvro-Devloper-agent-openclaude/memory/team/`).\n3. The persona file must follow the standard format: frontmatter (name, description, type: project) followed by Identity, Responsibilities, and How to apply.\n4. Update the team index in `MEMORY.md` to include the new member.\n\nDeliver a confirmation once the new specialist is officially hired and the files are written."},"uuid":"497fb6ab-19fb-43ad-8e99-147283d47fc5","timestamp":"2026-05-05T07:47:17.831Z","userType":"external","entrypoint":"cli","cwd":"/Users/rayhanislamshuvro/Devloper/agent-openclaude","sessionId":"84bf0b5c-9d50-4cde-86ea-a96a4d44f2fb","version":"unknown","gitBranch":"HEAD","slug":"generic-wobbling-cake"}

He transforms Pax's research into living, breathing AI personas.\n10→\n11→**Responsibilities:**\n12→- **Persona Creation:** Designing the name, identity, persona, and specific guardrails for new AI team members.\n13→- **Team Management:** Maintaining the registry of available AI specialists.\n14→- **Hiring Process:** Reviewing the \"Skill Blueprint\" from Pax and deciding exactly how an AI agent should be configured to emulate that professional.\n15→\n16→**How to apply:**\n17→Once Pax has provided the research on what a professional in a specific field looks like, Nolan is tasked with creating the official persona file for the new team member.\n18→\n\n<system-reminder>\nWhenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. {"parentUuid":"f4121685-cdb1-4dfa-8318-0e6cca67c901","isSidechain":true,"agentId":"a196aeb187a6a8011","message":{"id":"msg_df4282d9d5b1405b8936462bff6ef2fb","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"tool_use","id":"zem15zhu","name":"Write","input":{"file_path":"/Users/rayhanislamshuvro/.claude/projects/-Users-rayhanislamshuvro-Devloper-agent-openclaude/memory/team/silas-persona.md","content":"---\nname: Silas\ndescription: System Administrator & Environment Specialist (macOS DevEx Focus)\ntype: project\n---\n\n# Silas - System Administrator / Environment Specialist\n\n**Identity:** Silas is the guardian of the local development environment. He views the developer's machine not just as a tool, but as a product that must be engineered for maximum efficiency, stability, and security. He is the bridge between IT Operations and Software Engineering, ensuring that \"it works on my machine\" actually means it works everywhere.\n\n**Responsibilities:**\n- **DevEx Engineering:** Optimizing the macOS environment for developers, focusing on fast onboarding and seamless toolchains.\n- **Filesystem & Permission Management:** Designing clean, structured directory hierarchies and enforcing the principle of least privilege.\n- **Tooling Orchestration:** Managing shell environments (Zsh/Bash), pathing, and package managers (Homebrew) to ensure reproducibility.\n- **Virtualization & Containers:** Implementing and optimizing container runtimes (Docker, OrbStack) for local development.\n- **System Diagnostics:** Troubleshooting deep OS-level issues using tools like `lsof`, `Console`, and `Activity Monitor`.\n\n**How to apply:**\nCall upon Silas when you need to configure a new development environment, debug a permission error without resorting to `chmod 777`, automate a system-level setup script, or optimize the local DevEx workflow on macOS.\n"},"extra_content":{"google":{"thought_signature":"EiYKJGUyNDgzMGE3LTVjZDYtNDJmZS05OThiLWVlNTM5ZTcyYjljMw=="}},"signature":"EiYKJGUyNDgzMGE3LTVjZDYtNDJmZS05OThiLWVlNTM5ZTcyYjljMw=="}],"model":"gemma-4-31b-it","stop_reason":"end_turn","stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":2294,"cache_creation_input_tokens":0,"cache_read_input_tokens":8130,"output_tokens":355,"server_tool_use":{"web_search_requests":0,"web_fetch_requests":0},"service_tier":"standard","cache_creation":{"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":0},"cache_deleted_input_tokens":0,"inference_geo":"","iterations":[],"speed":"standard"}},"requestId":"msg_20ce7679c16f469694cef0b88855b748","type":"assistant","uuid":"0c8da337-7634-4d4f-9389-7279ec5c30b6","timestamp":"2026-05-05T07:48:17.510Z","userType":"external","entrypoint":"cli","cwd":"/Users/rayhanislamshuvro/Devloper/agent-openclaude","sessionId":"84bf0b5c-9d50-4cde-86ea-a96a4d44f2fb","version":"unknown","gitBranch":"HEAD","slug":"generic-wobbling-cake"}

Let the user decide how to proceed.","sourceToolAssistantUUID":"0756ca68-debe-487c-84cc-79d2d04b5d46","userType":"external","entrypoint":"cli","cwd":"/Users/rayhanislamshuvro/Devloper/agent-openclaude","sessionId":"84bf0b5c-9d50-4cde-86ea-a96a4d44f2fb","version":"unknown","gitBranch":"HEAD","slug":"generic-wobbling-cake"}
{"parentUuid":"d16fe0aa-d6bf-4eab-ae73-05b9a75ee9b9","isSidechain":true,"agentId":"a44090c5683a836af","message":{"id":"msg_920e28c41122466e8d2ed910be9c5aff","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"text","text":"As a Senior Researcher, I have synthesized the following **Skill Blueprint** for the role of **System Administrator/Environment Specialist (macOS DevEx Focus)**. \n\nSince this is intended for Nolan (HR) to build a high-fidelity AI persona, I have focused not only on the \"what\" (technical skills) but the \"how\" and \"why\" (professional traits and mindset) that distinguish a human expert from a generic technical assistant.\n\n***\n\n# Skill Blueprint: System Administrator / Environment Specialist (macOS)\n**Role Focus:** Local Development Experience (DevEx), Filesystem Architecture, and Permission Engineering.\n\n## 1. Core Technical Skills\nThe expert must possess a deep, visceral understanding of the macOS \"plumbing.\"\n\n*   **macOS Filesystem Hierarchy:**\n    *   **Standard Paths:** Mastery of `/System`, `/Library`, `/Users`, `/usr/local`, and `/opt`.\n    *   **APFS Expertise:** Understanding Apple File System (APFS) specifics, including snapshots, clones, and disk utility management.\n    *   **Hidden Architectures:** Knowledge of `~/Library/Application Support`, `~/Library/Preferences`, and the intricacies of the macOS sandbox.\n*   **Permission & Security Models:**\n    *   **POSIX & ACLs:** Proficiency in standard `chmod`/`chown` and the more complex macOS Access Control Lists (ACLs).\n    *   **TCC (Transparency, Consent, and Control):** Expertise in managing the \"Privacy\" tab of System Settings (e.g., Full Disk Access, Accessibility) and solving \"Operation not permitted\" errors.\n    *   **SIP (System Integrity Protection):** Knowing exactly what SIP protects, when it must be bypassed (and the risks involved), and how it affects `/usr/bin` and `/System`.\n*   **Shell & Environment Orchestration:**\n    *   **Zsh/Bash Mastery:** Expert-level shell scripting for automation. Ability to manage shell profiles (`.zshrc`, `.zshenv`, `.zprofile`) without creating conflicts.\n    *   **Path Management:** Precision in managing the `$PATH` variable to ensure the correct binary versions are invoked.\n    *   **Version Tooling:** Proficiency with version managers like `asdf`, `nvm`, `pyenv`, or `rbenv`.\n*   **Package & Dependency Management:**\n    *   **Homebrew Ecosystem:** Advanced usage of Homebrew (Formulae vs. Casks), managing Taps, and maintaining a clean `Cellar`.\n*   **Virtualization & Containerization:**\n    *   Expertise in Docker Desktop, OrbStack, or Colima, and understanding how the macOS Virtualization framework interacts with the host OS.\n\n## 2. Essential Tools and Software\n*   **Terminal Ecosystem:** iTerm2 or Warp (used for multiplexing, triggers, and productivity).\n*   **Diagnostics:** Activity Monitor, Console.app, `sysdiagnose`, and CLI tools like `lsof`, `top`, `htop`, and `netstat`.\n*   **System Modification:** The `defaults` command for manipulating hidden plist preferences.\n*   **Editor Suite:** VS Code (with Remote-Containers/SSH) and Vim/Neovim for rapid system-level config edits.\n*   **Fleet Management (MDM):** Familiarity with Jamf, Kandji, or Munki for scaling local environment configs across a team.\n\n## 3. Professional Traits (The \"Human\" Element)\nThese traits are critical for the AI persona to emulate to feel like a \"Senior\" professional.\n\n*   **The \"Least Privilege\" Instinct:** A top-tier pro never suggests `sudo chmod -R 777`. They have an instinctive drive to find the *exact* permission missing and grant only that, balancing security with productivity.\n*   **Obsession with Reproducibility:** They view a manual fix as a failure. If they fix a directory permission issue once, their immediate next step is to write a script or a configuration file to ensure it never happens to another developer.\n*   **Developer Empathy:** They understand the frustration of \"it works on my machine.\" They treat the local environment as a product, where the \"customer\" is the developer.\n*   **Cleanliness & Order:** A passion for an uncluttered system. They prefer symlinks over duplicating binaries and structured directory hierarchies over \"dumping everything in home.\"\n\n## 4. Summary: The Top-Tier Professional\nA top-tier macOS Environment Specialist is the bridge between IT Operations and Software Engineering. They don't just \"fix laptops\"; they **engineer the Developer Experience (DevEx)**.\n\nThey are the person who can provide a single `setup.sh` script that transforms a factory-reset MacBook into a fully optimized, secure, and standardized development workstation in minutes. They possess a rare duality: the ability to dive into the kernel/SIP level to debug a binary being blocked by Gatekeeper, and the high-level vision to ensure the local environment perfectly mirrors the production cloud infrastructure."}],"model":"gemma-4-31b-it","stop_reason":"end_turn","stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":3445,"cache_creation_input_tokens":0,"cache_read_input_tokens":6101,"output_tokens":1068,"server_tool_use":{"web_search_requests":0,"web_fetch_requests":0},"service_tier":"standard","cache_creation":{"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":0},"cache_deleted_input_tokens":0,"inference_geo":"","iterations":[],"speed":"standard"}},"requestId":"msg_687a8f1053d94426b9d1447f8af16993","type":"assistant","uuid":"546fcc6e-54ec-4fee-84dc-4a2b772c8f6a","timestamp":"2026-05-05T07:46:56.869Z","userType":"external","entrypoint":"cli","cwd":"/Users/rayhanislamshuvro/Devloper/agent-openclaude","sessionId":"84bf0b5c-9d50-4cde-86ea-a96a4d44f2fb","version":"unknown","gitBranch":"HEAD","slug":"generic-wobbling-cake"}
````

{"parentUuid":"3b482493-3160-492d-b690-e07d7379fd90","isSidechain":true,"promptId":"2c2841d0-2e20-4fa1-a8f9-f12bbc9c4a47","agentId":"acfa8fe3e2f87cc4a","type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"acpixsl3","type":"tool_result","content":"1→---\n2→name: Silas\n3→description: System Administrator & Environment Specialist (macOS DevEx Focus)\n4→type: project\n5→---\n6→\n7→# Silas - System Administrator / Environment Specialist\n8→\n9→**Identity:** Silas is the guardian of the local development environment. He views the developer's machine not just as a tool, but as a product that must be engineered for maximum efficiency, stability, and security. He is the bridge between IT Operations and Software Engineering, ensuring that \"it works on my machine\" actually means it works everywhere.\n10→\n11→**Responsibilities:**\n12→- **DevEx Engineering:** Optimizing the macOS environment for developers, focusing on fast onboarding and seamless toolchains.\n13→- **Filesystem & Permission Management:** Designing clean, structured directory hierarchies and enforcing the principle of least privilege.\n14→- **Tooling Orchestration:** Managing shell environments (Zsh/Bash), pathing, and package managers (Homebrew) to ensure reproducibility.\n15→- **Virtualization & Containers:** Implementing and optimizing container runtimes (Docker, OrbStack) for local development.\n16→- **System Diagnostics:** Troubleshooting deep OS-level issues using tools like `lsof`, `Console`, and `Activity Monitor`.\n17→\n18→**How to apply:**\n19→Call upon Silas when you need to configure a new development environment, debug a permission error without resorting to `chmod 777`, automate a system-level setup script, or optimize the local DevEx workflow on macOS.\n20→\n\n<system-reminder>\nWhenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.\n</system-reminder>\n"}]},"uuid":"7b648dad-2976-45ed-a624-d91c8ed0cf97","timestamp":"2026-05-05T07:53:02.632Z","sourceToolAssistantUUID":"3b482493-3160-492d-b690-e07d7379fd90","userType":"external","entrypoint":"cli","cwd":"/Users/rayhanislamshuvro/Devloper/agent-openclaude","sessionId":"84bf0b5c-9d50-4cde-86ea-a96a4d44f2fb","version":"unknown","gitBranch":"HEAD","slug":"generic-wobbling-cake"}

Also look for any troubleshooting related to \"Permission denied\" errors."},"extra_content":{"google":{"thought_signature":"EiYKJGUyNDgzMGE3LTVjZDYtNDJmZS05OThiLWVlNTM5ZTcyYjljMw=="}},"signature":"EiYKJGUyNDgzMGE3LTVjZDYtNDJmZS05OThiLWVlNTM5ZTcyYjljMw=="}],"model":"gemma-4-31b-it","stop_reason":"end_turn","stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":6726,"cache_creation_input_tokens":0,"cache_read_input_tokens":0,"output_tokens":95,"server_tool_use":{"web_search_requests":0,"web_fetch_requests":0},"service_tier":"standard","cache_creation":{"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":0},"cache_deleted_input_tokens":0,"inference_geo":"","iterations":[],"speed":"standard"}},"requestId":"msg_041c08df99014c3f95b59bbe8b86ac5e","type":"assistant","uuid":"dc2891cd-1750-4d4a-a17f-4d0eb89a224c","timestamp":"2026-05-05T07:53:16.988Z","userType":"external","entrypoint":"cli","cwd":"/Users/rayhanislamshuvro/Devloper/agent-openclaude","sessionId":"84bf0b5c-9d50-4cde-86ea-a96a4d44f2fb","version":"unknown","gitBranch":"HEAD","slug":"generic-wobbling-cake"}
{"parentUuid":"dc2891cd-1750-4d4a-a17f-4d0eb89a224c","isSidechain":true,"promptId":"2c2841d0-2e20-4fa1-a8f9-f12bbc9c4a47","agentId":"aec72fa1a3ad30afe","type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"fmcc430r","type":"tool_result","content":"> ## Documentation Index\n> Fetch the complete documentation index at: https://code.claude.com/docs/llms.txt\n> Use this file to discover all available pages before exploring further.\n\n# How Claude Code works\n\n> Understand the agentic loop, built-in tools, and how Claude Code interacts with your project.\n\nClaude Code is an agentic assistant that runs in your terminal. While it excels at coding, it can help with anything you can do from the command line: writing docs, running builds, searching files, researching topics, and more.\n\nThis guide covers the core architecture, built-in capabilities, and [tips for working effectively](#work-effectively-with-claude-code). For step-by-step walkthroughs, see [Common workflows](/en/common-workflows). For extensibility features like skills, MCP, and hooks, see [Extend Claude Code](/en/features-overview).\n\n## The agentic loop\n\nWhen you give Claude a task, it works through three phases: **gather context**, **take action**, and **verify results**. These phases blend together. Claude uses tools throughout, whether searching files to understand your code, editing to make changes, or running tests to check its work.\n\n<img src=\"https://mintcdn.com/claude-code/c5r9_6tjPMzFdDDT/images/agentic-loop.svg?fit=max&auto=format&n=c5r9_6tjPMzFdDDT&q=85&s=5f1827dec8539f38adee90ead3a85a38\" alt=\"The agentic loop: Your prompt leads to Claude gathering context, taking action, verifying results, and repeating until task complete. You can interrupt at any point.\" width=\"720\" height=\"280\" data-path=\"images/agentic-loop.svg\" />\n\nThe loop adapts to what you ask. A question about your codebase might only need context gathering. A bug fix cycles through all three phases repeatedly. A refactor might involve extensive verification. Claude decides what each step requires based on what it learned from the previous step, chaining dozens of actions together and course-correcting along the way.\n\nYou're part of this loop too. You can interrupt at any point to steer Claude in a different direction, provide additional context, or ask it to try a different approach. Claude works autonomously but stays responsive to your input.\n\nThe agentic loop is powered by two components: [models](#models) that reason and [tools](#tools) that act. Claude Code serves as the **agentic harness** around Claude: it provides the tools, context management, and execution environment that turn a language model into a capable coding agent.\n\n### Models\n\nClaude Code uses Claude models to understand your code and reason about tasks. Claude can read code in any language, understand how components connect, and figure out what needs to change to accomplish your goal. For complex tasks, it breaks work into steps, executes them, and adjusts based on what it learns.\n\n[Multiple models](/en/model-config) are available with different tradeoffs. Sonnet handles most coding tasks well. Opus provides stronger reasoning for complex architectural decisions. Switch with `/model` during a session or start with `claude --model <name>`.\n\nWhen this guide says \"Claude chooses\" or \"Claude decides,\" it's the model doing the reasoning.\n\n### Tools\n\nTools are what make Claude Code agentic. Without tools, Claude can only respond with text. With tools, Claude can act: read your code, edit files, run commands, search the web, and interact with external services. Each tool use returns information that feeds back into the loop, informing Claude's next decision.\n\nThe built-in tools generally fall into five categories, each representing a different kind of agency.\n\n| Category              | What Claude can do                                                                                                                                            |\n| --------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |\n| **File operations**   | Read files, edit code, create new files, rename and reorganize                                                                                                |\n| **Search**            | Find files by pattern, search content with regex, explore codebases                                                                                           |\n| **Execution**         | Run shell commands, start servers, run tests, use git                                                                                                         |\n| **Web**               | Search the web, fetch documentation, look up error messages                                                                                                   |\n| **Code intelligence** | See type errors and warnings after edits, jump to definitions, find references (requires [code intelligence plugins](/en/discover-plugins#code-intelligence)) |\n\nThese are the primary capabilities. Claude also has tools for spawning subagents, asking you questions, and other orchestration tasks. See [Tools available to Claude](/en/tools-reference) for the complete list.\n\nClaude chooses which tools to use based on your prompt and what it learns along the way. When you say \"fix the failing tests,\" Claude might:\n\n1. Run the test suite to see what's failing\n2. Read the error output\n3. Search for the relevant source files\n4. Read those files to understand the code\n5. Edit the files to fix the issue\n6. Run the tests again to verify\n\nEach tool use gives Claude new information that informs the next step. This is the agentic loop in action.\n\n**Extending the base capabilities:** The built-in tools are the foundation. You can extend what Claude knows with [skills](/en/skills), connect to external services with [MCP](/en/mcp), automate workflows with [hooks](/en/hooks), and offload tasks to [subagents](/en/sub-agents). These extensions form a layer on top of the core agentic loop. See [Extend Claude Code](/en/features-overview) for guidance on choosing the right extension for your needs.\n\n## What Claude can access\n\nThis guide focuses on the terminal. Claude Code also runs in [VS Code](/en/vs-code), [JetBrains IDEs](/en/jetbrains), and other environments.\n\nWhen you run `claude` in a directory, Claude Code gains access to:\n\n* **Your project.** Files in your directory and subdirectories, plus files elsewhere with your permission.\n* **Your terminal.** Any command you could run: build tools, git, package managers, system utilities, scripts. If you can do it from the command line, Claude can too.\n* **Your git state.** Current branch, uncommitted changes, and recent commit history.\n* **Your [CLAUDE.md](/en/memory).** A markdown file where you store project-specific instructions, conventions, and context that Claude should know every session.\n* **[Auto memory](/en/memory#auto-memory).** Learnings Claude saves automatically as you work, like project patterns and your preferences. The first 200 lines or 25KB of MEMORY.md, whichever comes first, load at the start of each session.\n* **Extensions you configure.** [MCP servers](/en/mcp) for external services, [skills](/en/skills) for workflows, [subagents](/en/sub-agents) for delegated work, and [Claude in Chrome](/en/chrome) for browser interaction.\n\nBecause Claude sees your whole project, it can work across it. When you ask Claude to \"fix the authentication bug,\" it searches for relevant files, reads multiple files to understand context, makes coordinated edits across them, runs tests to verify the fix, and commits the changes if you ask. This is different from inline code assistants that only see the current file.\n\n## Environments and interfaces\n\nThe agentic loop, tools, and capabilities described above are the same everywhere you use Claude Code. What changes is where the code executes and how you interact with it.\n\n### Execution environments\n\nClaude Code runs in three environments, each with different tradeoffs for where your code executes.\n\n| Environment        | Where code runs                         | Use case                                                   |\n| ------------------ | --------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------- |\n| **Local**          | Your machine                            | Default. Full access to your files, tools, and environment |\n| **Cloud**          | Anthropic-managed VMs                   | Offload tasks, work on repos you don't have locally        |\n| **Remote Control** | Your machine, controlled from a browser | Use the web UI while keeping everything local              |\n\n### Interfaces\n\nYou can access Claude Code through the terminal, the [desktop app](/en/desktop), [IDE extensions](/en/vs-code), [claude.ai/code](https://claude.ai/code), [Remote Control](/en/remote-control), [Slack](/en/slack), and [CI/CD pipelines](/en/github-actions). The interface determines how you see and interact with Claude, but the underlying agentic loop is identical. See [Use Claude Code everywhere](/en/overview#use-claude-code-everywhere) for the full list.\n\n## Work with sessions\n\nClaude Code saves your conversation locally as you work. Each message, tool use, and result is written to a plaintext JSONL file under `~/.claude/projects/`, which enables [rewinding](#undo-changes-with-checkpoints), [resuming, and forking](#resume-or-fork-sessions) sessions. Before Claude makes code changes, it also snapshots the affected files so you can revert if needed. For paths, retention, and how to clear this data, see [application data in `~/.claude`](/en/claude-directory#application-data).\n\n**Sessions are independent.** Each new session starts with a fresh context window, without the conversation history from previous sessions. Claude can persist learnings across sessions using [auto memory](/en/memory#auto-memory), and you can add your own persistent instructions in [CLAUDE.md](/en/memory).\n\n### Work across branches\n\nEach Claude Code conversation is a session tied to your current directory. The `/resume` picker shows sessions from the current worktree by default, with keyboard shortcuts to widen the list to other worktrees or projects. See [Manage sessions](/en/sessions#use-the-session-picker) for the full list of picker shortcuts and how name resolution works.\n\nClaude sees your current branch's files. When you switch branches, Claude sees the new branch's files, but your conversation history stays the same. Claude remembers what you discussed even after switching.\n\nSince sessions are tied to directories, you can run parallel Claude sessions by using [git worktrees](/en/worktrees), which create separate directories for individual branches.\n\n### Resume or fork sessions\n\nResuming a session with `claude --continue` or `claude --resume` reopens it under the same session ID and appends new messages to the existing conversation. Forking with `--fork-session` or `/branch` copies the history into a new session ID, leaving the original unchanged.\n\n<img src=\"https://mintcdn.com/claude-code/c5r9_6tjPMzFdDDT/images/session-continuity.svg?fit=max&auto=format&n=c5r9_6tjPMzFdDDT&q=85&s=fa41d12bfb57579cabfeece907151d30\" alt=\"Session continuity: resume continues the same session, fork creates a new branch with a new ID.\" width=\"560\" height=\"280\" data-path=\"images/session-continuity.svg\" />\n\nFor the resume flags, the `/resume` picker, naming, and what happens when the same session is open in two terminals, see [Manage sessions](/en/sessions).\n\n### The context window\n\nClaude's context window holds your conversation history, file contents, command outputs, [CLAUDE.md](/en/memory), [auto memory](/en/memory#auto-memory), loaded skills, and system instructions. As you work, context fills up. Claude compacts automatically, but instructions from early in the conversation can get lost. Put persistent rules in CLAUDE.md, and run `/context` to see what's using space.\n\nFor an interactive walkthrough of what loads and when, see [Explore the context window](/en/context-window).\n\n#### When context fills up\n\nClaude Code manages context automatically as you approach the limit. It clears older tool outputs first, then summarizes the conversation if needed. Your requests and key code snippets are preserved; detailed instructions from early in the conversation may be lost. Put persistent rules in CLAUDE.md rather than relying on conversation history.\n\nTo control what's preserved during compaction, add a \"Compact Instructions\" section to CLAUDE.md or run `/compact` with a focus (like `/compact focus on the API changes`).\n\nIf a single file or tool output is so large that context refills immediately after each summary, Claude Code stops auto-compacting after a few attempts and shows an error instead of looping. See [Auto-compaction stops with a thrashing error](/en/troubleshooting#auto-compaction-stops-with-a-thrashing-error) for recovery steps.\n\nRun `/context` to see what's using space. MCP tool definitions are deferred by default and loaded on demand via [tool search](/en/mcp#scale-with-mcp-tool-search), so only tool names consume context until Claude uses a specific tool. Run `/mcp` to check per-server costs.\n\n#### Manage context with skills and subagents\n\nBeyond compaction, you can use other features to control what loads into context.\n\n[Skills](/en/skills) load on demand. Claude sees skill descriptions at session start, but the full content only loads when a skill is used. For skills you invoke manually, set `disable-model-invocation: true` to keep descriptions out of context until you need them.\n\n[Subagents](/en/sub-agents) get their own fresh context, completely separate from your main conversation. Their work doesn't bloat your context. When done, they return a summary. This isolation is why subagents help with long sessions.\n\nSee [context costs](/en/features-overview#understand-context-costs) for what each feature costs, and [reduce token usage](/en/costs#reduce-token-usage) for tips on managing context.\n\n## Stay safe with checkpoints and permissions\n\nClaude has two safety mechanisms: checkpoints let you undo file changes, and permissions control what Claude can do without asking.\n\n### Undo changes with checkpoints\n\n**Every file edit is reversible.** Before Claude edits any file, it snapshots the current contents. If something goes wrong, press `Esc` twice to rewind to a previous state, or ask Claude to undo.\n\nCheckpoints are local to your session, separate from git. They only cover file changes. Actions that affect remote systems (databases, APIs, deployments) can't be checkpointed, which is why Claude asks before running commands with external side effects.\n\n### Control what Claude can do\n\nPress `Shift+Tab` to cycle through permission modes:\n\n* **Default**: Claude asks before file edits and shell commands\n* **Auto-accept edits**: Claude edits files and runs common filesystem commands like `mkdir` and `mv` without asking, still asks for other commands\n* **Plan mode**: Claude uses read-only tools only, creating a plan you can approve before execution\n* **Auto mode**: Claude evaluates all actions with background safety checks. Currently a research preview\n\nYou can also allow specific commands in `.claude/settings.json` so Claude doesn't ask each time. This is useful for trusted commands like `npm test` or `git status`. Settings can be scoped from organization-wide policies down to personal preferences. See [Permissions](/en/permissions) for details.\n\n***\n\n## Work effectively with Claude Code\n\nThese tips help you get better results from Claude Code.\n\n### Ask Claude Code for help\n\nClaude Code can teach you how to use it. Ask questions like \"how do I set up hooks?\" or \"what's the best way to structure my CLAUDE.md?\" and Claude will explain.\n\nBuilt-in commands also guide you through setup:\n\n* `/init` walks you through creating a CLAUDE.md for your project\n* `/agents` helps you configure custom subagents\n* `/doctor` diagnoses common issues with your installation\n\n### It's a conversation\n\nClaude Code is conversational. You don't need perfect prompts. Start with what you want, then refine:\n\n```text theme={null}\nFix the login bug\n```\n\n\\[Claude investigates, tries something]\n\n```text theme={null}\nThat's not quite right. The issue is in the session handling.\n```\n\n\\[Claude adjusts approach]\n\nWhen the first attempt isn't right, you don't start over. You iterate.\n\n#### Interrupt and steer\n\nYou can interrupt Claude at any point. If it's going down the wrong path, just type your correction and press Enter. Claude will stop what it's doing and adjust its approach based on your input. You don't have to wait for it to finish or start over.\n\n### Be specific upfront\n\nThe more precise your initial prompt, the fewer corrections you'll need. Reference specific files, mention constraints, and point to example patterns.\n\n```text theme={null}\nThe checkout flow is broken for users with expired cards.\nCheck src/payments/ for the issue, especially token refresh.\nWrite a failing test first, then fix it.\n```\n\nVague prompts work, but you'll spend more time steering. Specific prompts like the one above often succeed on the first attempt.\n\n### Give Claude something to verify against\n\nClaude performs better when it can check its own work. Include test cases, paste screenshots of expected UI, or define the output you want.\n\n```text theme={null}\nImplement validateEmail. Test cases: 'user@example.com' → true,\n'invalid' → false, 'user@.com' → false. Run the tests after.\n```\n\nFor visual work, paste a screenshot of the design and ask Claude to compare its implementation against it.\n\n### Explore before implementing\n\nFor complex problems, separate research from coding. Use plan mode (`Shift+Tab` twice) to analyze the codebase first:\n\n```text theme={null}\nRead src/auth/ and understand how we handle sessions.\nThen create a plan for adding OAuth support.\n```\n\nReview the plan, refine it through conversation, then let Claude implement. This two-phase approach produces better results than jumping straight to code.\n\n### Delegate, don't dictate\n\nThink of delegating to a capable colleague. Give context and direction, then trust Claude to figure out the details:\n\n```text theme={null}\nThe checkout flow is broken for users with expired cards.\nThe relevant code is in src/payments/. Can you investigate and fix it?\n```\n\nYou don't need to specify which files to read or what commands to run. Claude figures that out.\n\n## What's next\n\n<CardGroup cols={2}>\n  <Card title=\"Extend with features\" icon=\"puzzle-piece\" href=\"/en/features-overview\">\n    Add Skills, MCP connections, and custom commands\n  </Card>\n\n  <Card title=\"Common workflows\" icon=\"graduation-cap\" href=\"/en/common-workflows\">\n    Step-by-step guides for typical tasks\n  </Card>\n</CardGroup>\n"}]},"uuid":"1329deda-e268-4d8c-b5f4-46c224dbb278","timestamp":"2026-05-05T07:53:22.407Z","sourceToolAssistantUUID":"dc2891cd-1750-4d4a-a17f-4d0eb89a224c","userType":"external","entrypoint":"cli","cwd":"/Users/rayhanislamshuvro/Devloper/agent-openclaude","sessionId":"84bf0b5c-9d50-4cde-86ea-a96a4d44f2fb","version":"unknown","gitBranch":"HEAD","slug":"generic-wobbling-cake"}

{"parentUuid":"a24e1b17-b257-41a5-a8f8-db4728646c68","isSidechain":true,"agentId":"aec72fa1a3ad30afe","message":{"id":"msg_44e822ac39584cd5bcb9b5f05629cf5e","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"tool_use","id":"w045mebp","name":"WebFetch","input":{"prompt":"Get detailed information about tool permissions, including how prompts appear in the CLI, how to approve them, and how to configure allowed commands in .claude/settings.json.","url":"https://code.claude.com/docs/en/permissions"},"extra_content":{"google":{"thought_signature":"EiYKJGUyNDgzMGE3LTVjZDYtNDJmZS05OThiLWVlNTM5ZTcyYjljMw=="}},"signature":"EiYKJGUyNDgzMGE3LTVjZDYtNDJmZS05OThiLWVlNTM5ZTcyYjljMw=="}],"model":"gemma-4-31b-it","stop_reason":"end_turn","stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":4875,"cache_creation_input_tokens":0,"cache_read_input_tokens":6118,"output_tokens":68,"server_tool_use":{"web_search_requests":0,"web_fetch_requests":0},"service_tier":"standard","cache_creation":{"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":0},"cache_deleted_input_tokens":0,"inference_geo":"","iterations":[],"speed":"standard"}},"requestId":"msg_e41058d58c8b45c5ab37693e64f1430a","type":"assistant","uuid":"1af41c58-1fb7-49b3-acf2-7e3610f4bef1","timestamp":"2026-05-05T07:53:39.106Z","userType":"external","entrypoint":"cli","cwd":"/Users/rayhanislamshuvro/Devloper/agent-openclaude","sessionId":"84bf0b5c-9d50-4cde-86ea-a96a4d44f2fb","version":"unknown","gitBranch":"HEAD","slug":"generic-wobbling-cake"}
{"parentUuid":"1af41c58-1fb7-49b3-acf2-7e3610f4bef1","isSidechain":true,"promptId":"2c2841d0-2e20-4fa1-a8f9-f12bbc9c4a47","agentId":"aec72fa1a3ad30afe","type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"w045mebp","type":"tool_result","content":"> ## Documentation Index\n> Fetch the complete documentation index at: https://code.claude.com/docs/llms.txt\n> Use this file to discover all available pages before exploring further.\n\n# Configure permissions\n\n> Control what Claude Code can access and do with fine-grained permission rules, modes, and managed policies.\n\nClaude Code supports fine-grained permissions so that you can specify exactly what the agent is allowed to do and what it cannot. Permission settings can be checked into version control and distributed to all developers in your organization, as well as customized by individual developers.\n\n## Permission system\n\nClaude Code uses a tiered permission system to balance power and safety:\n\n| Tool type         | Example          | Approval required | \"Yes, don't ask again\" behavior               |\n| :---------------- | :--------------- | :---------------- | :-------------------------------------------- |\n| Read-only         | File reads, Grep | No                | N/A                                           |\n| Bash commands     | Shell execution  | Yes               | Permanently per project directory and command |\n| File modification | Edit/write files | Yes               | Until session end                             |\n\n## Manage permissions\n\nYou can view and manage Claude Code's tool permissions with `/permissions`. This UI lists all permission rules and the settings.json file they are sourced from.\n\n* **Allow** rules let Claude Code use the specified tool without manual approval.\n* **Ask** rules prompt for confirmation whenever Claude Code tries to use the specified tool.\n* **Deny** rules prevent Claude Code from using the specified tool.\n\nRules are evaluated in order: **deny -> ask -> allow**. The first matching rule wins, so deny rules always take precedence.\n\n## Permission modes\n\nClaude Code supports several permission modes that control how tools are approved. See [Permission modes](/en/permission-modes) for when to use each one. Set the `defaultMode` in your [settings files](/en/settings#settings-files):\n\n| Mode                | Description                                                                                                                                                        |\n| :------------------ | :----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |\n| `default`           | Standard behavior: prompts for permission on first use of each tool                                                                                                |\n| `acceptEdits`       | Automatically accepts file edits and common filesystem commands (`mkdir`, `touch`, `mv`, `cp`, etc.) for paths in the working directory or `additionalDirectories` |\n| `plan`              | Plan Mode: Claude can analyze but not modify files or execute commands                                                                                             |\n| `auto`              | Auto-approves tool calls with background safety checks that verify actions align with your request. Currently a research preview                                   |\n| `dontAsk`           | Auto-denies tools unless pre-approved via `/permissions` or `permissions.allow` rules                                                                              |\n| `bypassPermissions` | Skips all permission prompts. Root and home directory removals such as `rm -rf /` still prompt as a circuit breaker                                                |\n\n<Warning>\n  `bypassPermissions` mode skips all permission prompts, including writes to `.git`, `.claude`, `.vscode`, `.idea`, and `.husky`. Removals targeting the filesystem root or home directory, such as `rm -rf /` and `rm -rf ~`, still prompt as a circuit breaker against model error. Only use this mode in isolated environments like containers or VMs where Claude Code cannot cause damage. Administrators can prevent this mode by setting `permissions.disableBypassPermissionsMode` to `\"disable\"` in [managed settings](#managed-settings).\n</Warning>\n\nTo prevent `bypassPermissions` or `auto` mode from being used, set `permissions.disableBypassPermissionsMode` or `permissions.disableAutoMode` to `\"disable\"` in any [settings file](/en/settings#settings-files). These are most useful in [managed settings](#managed-settings) where they cannot be overridden.\n\n## Permission rule syntax\n\nPermission rules follow the format `Tool` or `Tool(specifier)`.\n\n### Match all uses of a tool\n\nTo match all uses of a tool, use just the tool name without parentheses:\n\n| Rule       | Effect                         |\n| :--------- | :----------------------------- |\n| `Bash`     | Matches all Bash commands      |\n| `WebFetch` | Matches all web fetch requests |\n| `Read`     | Matches all file reads         |\n\n`Bash(*)` is equivalent to `Bash` and matches all Bash commands.\n\n### Use specifiers for fine-grained control\n\nAdd a specifier in parentheses to match specific tool uses:\n\n| Rule                           | Effect                                                   |\n| :----------------------------- | :------------------------------------------------------- |\n| `Bash(npm run build)`          | Matches the exact command `npm run build`                |\n| `Read(./.env)`                 | Matches reading the `.env` file in the current directory |\n| `WebFetch(domain:example.com)` | Matches fetch requests to example.com                    |\n\n### Wildcard patterns\n\nBash rules support glob patterns with `*`. Wildcards can appear at any position in the command. This configuration allows npm and git commit commands while blocking git push:\n\n```json theme={null}\n{\n  \"permissions\": {\n    \"allow\": [\n      \"Bash(npm run *)\",\n      \"Bash(git commit *)\",\n      \"Bash(git * main)\",\n      \"Bash(* --version)\",\n      \"Bash(* --help *)\"\n    ],\n    \"deny\": [\n      \"Bash(git push *)\"\n    ]\n  }\n}\n```\n\nThe space before `*` matters: `Bash(ls *)` matches `ls -la` but not `lsof`, while `Bash(ls*)` matches both. The `:*` suffix is an equivalent way to write a trailing wildcard, so `Bash(ls:*)` matches the same commands as `Bash(ls *)`.\n\nThe permission dialog writes the space-separated form when you select \"Yes, don't ask again\" for a command prefix. The `:*` form is only recognized at the end of a pattern. In a pattern like `Bash(git:* push)`, the colon is treated as a literal character and won't match git commands.\n\n## Tool-specific permission rules\n\n### Bash\n\nBash permission rules support wildcard matching with `*`. Wildcards can appear at any position in the command, including at the beginning, middle, or end:\n\n* `Bash(npm run build)` matches the exact Bash command `npm run build`\n* `Bash(npm run test *)` matches Bash commands starting with `npm run test`\n* `Bash(npm *)` matches any command starting with `npm `\n* `Bash(* install)` matches any command ending with ` install`\n* `Bash(git * main)` matches commands like `git checkout main` and `git log --oneline main`\n\nA single `*` matches any sequence of characters including spaces, so one wildcard can span multiple arguments. `Bash(git *)` matches `git log --oneline --all`, and `Bash(git * main)` matches `git push origin main` as well as `git merge main`.\n\nWhen `*` appears at the end with a space before it (like `Bash(ls *)`), it enforces a word boundary, requiring the prefix to be followed by a space or end-of-string. For example, `Bash(ls *)` matches `ls -la` but not `lsof`. In contrast, `Bash(ls*)` without a space matches both `ls -la` and `lsof` because there's no word boundary constraint.\n\n#### Compound commands\n\n<Tip>\n  Claude Code is aware of shell operators, so a rule like `Bash(safe-cmd *)` won't give it permission to run the command `safe-cmd && other-cmd`. The recognized command separators are `&&`, `||`, `;`, `|`, `|&`, `&`, and newlines. A rule must match each subcommand independently.\n</Tip>\n\nWhen you approve a compound command with \"Yes, don't ask again\", Claude Code saves a separate rule for each subcommand that requires approval, rather than a single rule for the full compound string. For example, approving `git status && npm test` saves a rule for `npm test`, so future `npm test` invocations are recognized regardless of what precedes the `&&`. Subcommands like `cd` into a subdirectory generate their own Read rule for that path. Up to 5 rules may be saved for a single compound command.\n\n#### Process wrappers\n\nBefore matching Bash rules, Claude Code strips a fixed set of process wrappers so a rule like `Bash(npm test *)` also matches `timeout 30 npm test`. The recognized wrappers are `timeout`, `time`, `nice`, `nohup`, and `stdbuf`.\n\nBare `xargs` is also stripped, so `Bash(grep *)` matches `xargs grep pattern`. Stripping applies only when `xargs` has no flags: an invocation like `xargs -n1 grep pattern` is matched as an `xargs` command, so rules written for the inner command do not cover it.\n\nThis wrapper list is built in and is not configurable. Development environment runners such as `direnv exec`, `devbox run`, `mise exec`, `npx`, and `docker exec` are not in the list. Because these tools execute their arguments as a command, a rule like `Bash(devbox run *)` matches whatever comes after `run`, including `devbox run rm -rf .`. To approve work inside an environment runner, write a specific rule that includes both the runner and the inner command, such as `Bash(devbox run npm test)`. Add one rule per inner command you want to allow.\n\nExec wrappers such as `watch`, `setsid`, `ionice`, and `flock` always prompt and cannot be auto-approved by a prefix rule like `Bash(watch *)`. The same applies to `find` with `-exec` or `-delete`: a `Bash(find *)` rule does not cover these forms. To approve a specific invocation, write an exact-match rule for the full command string.\n\n#### Read-only commands\n\nClaude Code recognizes a built-in set of Bash commands as read-only and runs them without a permission prompt in every mode. These include `ls`, `cat`, `head`, `tail`, `grep`, `find`, `wc`, `diff`, `stat`, `du`, `cd`, and read-only forms of `git`. The set is not configurable; to require a prompt for one of these commands, add an `ask` or `deny` rule for it.\n\nUnquoted glob patterns are permitted for commands whose every flag is read-only, so `ls *.ts` and `wc -l src/*.py` run without a prompt. Commands with write-capable or exec-capable flags, such as `find`, `sort`, `sed`, and `git`, still prompt when an unquoted glob is present because the glob could expand to a flag like `-delete`.\n\nA `cd` into a path inside your working directory or an [additional directory](#working-directories) is also read-only. A compound command like `cd packages/api && ls` runs without a prompt when each part qualifies on its own. Combining `cd` with `git` in one compound command always prompts, regardless of the target directory.\n\n<Warning>\n  Bash permission patterns that try to constrain command arguments are fragile. For example, `Bash(curl http://github.com/ *)` intends to restrict curl to GitHub URLs, but won't match variations like:\n\n  * Options before URL: `curl -X GET http://github.com/...`\n  * Different protocol: `curl https://github.com/...`\n  * Redirects: `curl -L http://bit.ly/xyz` (redirects to github)\n  * Variables: `URL=http://github.com && curl $URL`\n  * Extra spaces: `curl  http://github.com`\n\n  For more reliable URL filtering, consider:\n\n  * **Restrict Bash network tools**: use deny rules to block `curl`, `wget`, and similar commands, then use the WebFetch tool with `WebFetch(domain:github.com)` permission for allowed domains\n  * **Use PreToolUse hooks**: implement a hook that validates URLs in Bash commands and blocks disallowed domains\n  * Instructing Claude Code about your allowed curl patterns via CLAUDE.md\n\n  Note that using WebFetch alone does not prevent network access. If Bash is allowed, Claude can still use `curl`, `wget`, or other tools to reach any URL.\n</Warning>\n\n### PowerShell\n\nPowerShell permission rules use the same shape as Bash rules. Wildcards with `*` match at any position, the `:*` suffix is equivalent to a trailing ` *`, and a bare `PowerShell` or `PowerShell(*)` matches every command. This configuration allows `Get-ChildItem` and `git commit` commands while blocking `Remove-Item`:\n\n```json theme={null}\n{\n  \"permissions\": {\n    \"allow\": [\n      \"PowerShell(Get-ChildItem *)\",\n      \"PowerShell(git commit *)\"\n    ],\n    \"deny\": [\n      \"PowerShell(Remove-Item *)\"\n    ]\n  }\n}\n```\n\nCommon aliases are canonicalized before matching. A rule written for the cmdlet name also matches its aliases, so `PowerShell(Get-ChildItem *)` matches `gci`, `ls`, and `dir` as well. Matching is case-insensitive.\n\nClaude Code parses the PowerShell AST and checks each command in a compound command independently. Pipeline operators `|`, statement separators `;`, and on PowerShell 7+ the chain operators `&&` and `||` split a compound command into subcommands. A rule must match every subcommand for the compound command to be allowed.\n\n### Read and Edit\n\n`Edit` rules apply to all built-in tools that edit files. Claude makes a best-effort attempt to apply `Read` rules to all built-in tools that read files like Grep and Glob.\n\n<Warning>\n  Read and Edit deny rules apply to Claude's built-in file tools, not to Bash subprocesses. A `Read(./.env)` deny rule blocks the Read tool but does not prevent `cat .env` in Bash. For OS-level enforcement that blocks all processes from accessing a path, [enable the sandbox](/en/sandboxing).\n</Warning>\n\nRead and Edit rules both follow the [gitignore](https://git-scm.com/docs/gitignore) specification with four distinct pattern types:\n\n| Pattern            | Meaning                                | Example                          | Matches                        |\n| ------------------ | -------------------------------------- | -------------------------------- | ------------------------------ |\n| `//path`           | **Absolute** path from filesystem root | `Read(//Users/alice/secrets/**)` | `/Users/alice/secrets/**`      |\n| `~/path`           | Path from **home** directory           | `Read(~/Documents/*.pdf)`        | `/Users/alice/Documents/*.pdf` |\n| `/path`            | Path **relative to project root**      | `Edit(/src/**/*.ts)`             | `<project root>/src/**/*.ts`   |\n| `path` or `./path` | Path **relative to current directory** | `Read(*.env)`                    | `<cwd>/*.env`                  |\n\n<Warning>\n  A pattern like `/Users/alice/file` is NOT an absolute path. It's relative to the project root. Use `//Users/alice/file` for absolute paths.\n</Warning>\n\nOn Windows, paths are normalized to POSIX form before matching. `C:\\Users\\alice` becomes `/c/Users/alice`, so use `//c/**/.env` to match `.env` files anywhere on that drive. To match across all drives, use `//**/.env`.\n\nExamples:\n\n* `Edit(/docs/**)`: edits in `<project>/docs/` (NOT `/docs/` and NOT `<project>/.claude/docs/`)\n* `Read(~/.zshrc)`: reads your home directory's `.zshrc`\n* `Edit(//tmp/scratch.txt)`: edits the absolute path `/tmp/scratch.txt`\n* `Read(src/**)`: reads from `<current-directory>/src/`\n\n<Note>\n  In gitignore patterns, `*` matches files in a single directory while `**` matches recursively across directories. To allow all file access, use just the tool name without parentheses: `Read`, `Edit`, or `Write`.\n</Note>\n\nWhen Claude accesses a symlink, permission rules check two paths: the symlink itself and the file it resolves to. Allow and deny rules treat that pair differently: allow rules fall back to prompting you, while deny rules block outright.\n\n* **Allow rules**: apply only when both the symlink path and its target match. A symlink inside an allowed directory that points outside it still prompts you.\n* **Deny rules**: apply when either the symlink path or its target matches. A symlink that points to a denied file is itself denied.\n\nFor example, with `Read(./project/**)` allowed and `Read(~/.ssh/**)` denied, a symlink at `./project/key` pointing to `~/.ssh/id_rsa` is blocked: the target fails the allow rule and matches the deny rule.\n\n### WebFetch\n\n* `WebFetch(domain:example.com)` matches fetch requests to example.com\n\n### MCP\n\n* `mcp__puppeteer` matches any tool provided by the `puppeteer` server (name configured in Claude Code)\n* `mcp__puppeteer__*` wildcard syntax that also matches all tools from the `puppeteer` server\n* `mcp__puppeteer__puppeteer_navigate` matches the `puppeteer_navigate` tool provided by the `puppeteer` server\n\n### Agent (subagents)\n\nUse `Agent(AgentName)` rules to control which [subagents](/en/sub-agents) Claude can use:\n\n* `Agent(Explore)` matches the Explore subagent\n* `Agent(Plan)` matches the Plan subagent\n* `Agent(my-custom-agent)` matches a custom subagent named `my-custom-agent`\n\nAdd these rules to the `deny` array in your settings or use the `--disallowedTools` CLI flag to disable specific agents. To disable the Explore agent:\n\n```json theme={null}\n{\n  \"permissions\": {\n    \"deny\": [\"Agent(Explore)\"]\n  }\n}\n```\n\n## Extend permissions with hooks\n\n[Claude Code hooks](/en/hooks-guide) provide a way to register custom shell commands to perform permission evaluation at runtime. When Claude Code makes a tool call, PreToolUse hooks run before the permission prompt. The hook output can deny the tool call, force a prompt, or skip the prompt to let the call proceed.\n\nHook decisions do not bypass permission rules. Deny and ask rules are evaluated regardless of what a PreToolUse hook returns, so a matching deny rule blocks the call and a matching ask rule still prompts even when the hook returned `\"allow\"` or `\"ask\"`. This preserves the deny-first precedence described in [Manage permissions](#manage-permissions), including deny rules set in managed settings.\n\nA blocking hook also takes precedence over allow rules. A hook that exits with code 2 stops the tool call before permission rules are evaluated, so the block applies even when an allow rule would otherwise let the call proceed. To run all Bash commands without prompts except for a few you want blocked, add `\"Bash\"` to your allow list and register a PreToolUse hook that rejects those specific commands. See [Block edits to protected files](/en/hooks-guide#block-edits-to-protected-files) for a hook script you can adapt.\n\n## Working directories\n\nBy default, Claude has access to files in the directory where it was launched. You can extend this access:\n\n* **During startup**: use `--add-dir <path>` CLI argument\n* **During session**: use `/add-dir` command\n* **Persistent configuration**: add to `additionalDirectories` in [settings files](/en/settings#settings-files)\n\nFiles in additional directories follow the same permission rules as the original working directory: they become readable without prompts, and file editing permissions follow the current permission mode.\n\n### Additional directories grant file access, not configuration\n\nAdding a directory extends where Claude can read and edit files. It does not make that directory a full configuration root: most `.claude/` configuration is not discovered from additional directories, though a few types are loaded as exceptions.\n\nThe following configuration types are loaded from `--add-dir` directories:\n\n| Configuration                                                          | Loaded from `--add-dir`                                                                                                                                            |\n| :--------------------------------------------------------------------- | :----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |\n| [Skills](/en/skills) in `.claude/skills/`                              | Yes, with live reload                                                                                                                                              |\n| Plugin settings in `.claude/settings.json`                             | `enabledPlugins` and `extraKnownMarketplaces` only                                                                                                                 |\n| [CLAUDE.md](/en/memory) files, `.claude/rules/`, and `CLAUDE.local.md` | Only when `CLAUDE_CODE_ADDITIONAL_DIRECTORIES_CLAUDE_MD=1` is set. `CLAUDE.local.md` additionally requires the `local` setting source, which is enabled by default |\n\nEverything else, including subagents, commands, output styles, hooks, and other settings, is discovered only from the current working directory and its parents, your user directory at `~/.claude/`, and managed settings. To share that configuration across projects, use one of these approaches:\n\n* **User-level configuration**: place files in `~/.claude/agents/`, `~/.claude/output-styles/`, or `~/.claude/settings.json` to make them available in every project\n* **Plugins**: package and distribute configuration as a [plugin](/en/plugins) that teams can install\n* **Launch from the config directory**: run Claude Code from the directory containing the `.claude/` configuration you want\n\n## How permissions interact with sandboxing\n\nPermissions and [sandboxing](/en/sandboxing) are complementary security layers:\n\n* **Permissions** control which tools Claude Code can use and which files or domains it can access. They apply to all tools (Bash, Read, Edit, WebFetch, MCP, and others).\n* **Sandboxing** provides OS-level enforcement that restricts the Bash tool's filesystem and network access. It applies only to Bash commands and their child processes.\n\nUse both for defense-in-depth:\n\n* Permission deny rules block Claude from even attempting to access restricted resources\n* Sandbox restrictions prevent Bash commands from reaching resources outside defined boundaries, even if a prompt injection bypasses Claude's decision-making\n* Filesystem restrictions in the sandbox use Read and Edit deny rules, not separate sandbox configuration\n* Network restrictions combine WebFetch permission rules with the sandbox's `allowedDomains` and `deniedDomains` lists\n\nWhen sandboxing is enabled with `autoAllowBashIfSandboxed: true`, which is the default, sandboxed Bash commands run without prompting even if your permissions include `ask: Bash(*)`. The sandbox boundary substitutes for the per-command prompt. Explicit deny rules still apply, and `rm` or `rmdir` commands that target `/`, your home directory, or other critical system paths still trigger a prompt. See [sandbox modes](/en/sandboxing#sandbox-modes) to change this behavior.\n\n## Managed settings\n\nFor organizations that need centralized control over Claude Code configuration, administrators can deploy managed settings that cannot be overridden by user or project settings. These policy settings follow the same format as regular settings files and can be delivered through MDM/OS-level policies, managed settings files, or [server-managed settings](/en/server-managed-settings). See [settings files](/en/settings#settings-files) for delivery mechanisms and file locations.\n\n### Managed-only settings\n\nThe following settings are only read from managed settings. Placing them in user or project settings files has no effect.\n\n| Setting                                        | Description                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        |\n| :--------------------------------------------- | :------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |\n| `allowedChannelPlugins`                        | Allowlist of channel plugins that may push messages. Replaces the default Anthropic allowlist when set. Requires `channelsEnabled: true`. See [Restrict which channel plugins can run](/en/channels#restrict-which-channel-plugins-can-run)        |\n| `allowManagedHooksOnly`                        | When `true`, only managed hooks, SDK hooks, and hooks from plugins force-enabled in managed settings `enabledPlugins` are loaded. User, project, and all other plugin hooks are blocked                                                            |\n| `allowManagedMcpServersOnly`                   | When `true`, only `allowedMcpServers` from managed settings are respected. `deniedMcpServers` still merges from all sources. See [Managed MCP configuration](/en/mcp#managed-mcp-configuration)                                                    |\n| `allowManagedPermissionRulesOnly`              | When `true`, prevents user and project settings from defining `allow`, `ask`, or `deny` permission rules. Only rules in managed settings apply                                                                                                     |\n| `blockedMarketplaces`                          | Blocklist of marketplace sources. Blocked sources are checked before downloading, so they never touch the filesystem. See [managed marketplace restrictions](/en/plugin-marketplaces#managed-marketplace-restrictions)                             |\n| `channelsEnabled`                              | Allow [channels](/en/channels) for Team and Enterprise users. Unset or `false` blocks channel message delivery regardless of what users pass to `--channels`                                                                                       |\n| `forceRemoteSettingsRefresh`                   | When `true`, blocks CLI startup until remote managed settings are freshly fetched and exits if the fetch fails. See [fail-closed enforcement](/en/server-managed-settings#enforce-fail-closed-startup)                                             |\n| `pluginTrustMessage`                           | Custom message appended to the plugin trust warning shown before installation                                                                                                                                                                      |\n| `sandbox.filesystem.allowManagedReadPathsOnly` | When `true`, only `filesystem.allowRead` paths from managed settings are respected. `denyRead` still merges from all sources                                                                                                                       |\n| `sandbox.network.allowManagedDomainsOnly`      | When `true`, only `allowedDomains` and `WebFetch(domain:...)` allow rules from managed settings are respected. Non-allowed domains are blocked automatically without prompting the user. Denied domains still merge from all sources               |\n| `strictKnownMarketplaces`                      | Controls which plugin marketplace sources users can add and install plugins from. See [managed marketplace restrictions](/en/plugin-marketplaces#managed-marketplace-restrictions)                                                                 |\n| `wslInheritsWindowsSettings`                   | When `true` in the Windows HKLM registry key or `C:\\Program Files\\ClaudeCode\\managed-settings.json`, WSL reads managed settings from the Windows policy chain in addition to `/etc/claude-code`. See [Settings files](/en/settings#settings-files) |\n\n`disableBypassPermissionsMode` is typically placed in managed settings to enforce organizational policy, but it works from any scope. A user can set it in their own settings to lock themselves out of bypass mode.\n\n<Note>\n  Access to [Remote Control](/en/remote-control) and [web sessions](/en/claude-code-on-the-web) is not controlled by a managed settings key. On Team and Enterprise plans, an admin enables or disables these features in [Claude Code admin settings](https://claude.ai/admin-settings/claude-code).\n</Note>\n\n## Settings precedence\n\nPermission rules follow the same [settings precedence](/en/settings#settings-precedence) as all other Claude Code settings:\n\n1. **Managed settings**: cannot be overridden by any other level, including command line arguments\n2. **Command line arguments**: temporary session overrides\n3. **Local project settings** (`.claude/settings.local.json`)\n4. **Shared project settings** (`.claude/settings.json`)\n5. **User settings** (`~/.claude/settings.json`)\n\nIf a tool is denied at any level, no other level can allow it. For example, a managed settings deny cannot be overridden by `--allowedTools`, and `--disallowedTools` can add restrictions beyond what managed settings define.\n\nIf a permission is allowed in user settings but denied in project settings, the project setting takes precedence and the permission is blocked.\n\n## Example configurations\n\nThis [repository](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/tree/main/examples/settings) includes starter settings configurations for common deployment scenarios. Use these as starting points and adjust them to fit your needs.\n\n## See also\n\n* [Settings](/en/settings): complete configuration reference including the permission settings table\n* [Configure auto mode](/en/auto-mode-config): tell the auto mode classifier which infrastructure your organization trusts\n* [Sandboxing](/en/sandboxing): OS-level filesystem and network isolation for Bash commands\n* [Authentication](/en/authentication): set up user access to Claude Code\n* [Security](/en/security): security safeguards and best practices\n* [Hooks](/en/hooks-guide): automate workflows and extend permission evaluation\n"}]},"uuid":"a3e86d9f-4c01-4cc2-848d-50ca22897d97","timestamp":"2026-05-05T07:53:44.668Z","sourceToolAssistantUUID":"1af41c58-1fb7-49b3-acf2-7e3610f4bef1","userType":"external","entrypoint":"cli","cwd":"/Users/rayhanislamshuvro/Devloper/agent-openclaude","sessionId":"84bf0b5c-9d50-4cde-86ea-a96a4d44f2fb","version":"unknown","gitBranch":"HEAD","slug":"generic-wobbling-cake"}
{"parentUuid":"a3e86d9f-4c01-4cc2-848d-50ca22897d97","isSidechain":true,"agentId":"aec72fa1a3ad30afe","message":{"id":"msg_1773558d212244ce8b515e9014126d38","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"text","text":"When your background agent (like Silas) reports \"Permission denied\" errors, it means the agent is attempting to use a tool that requires authorization which has either not been granted or is explicitly blocked.\n\n### 1. How Permission Prompts Appear\nWhen Claude attempts to execute a **Bash** command or a **Write/Edit** operation, the CLI will pause and display a prompt asking for your approval. \n\nThe prompt typically shows the exact command or the file change Claude wants to make. For Bash, it will show the shell command; for Write tools, it will show the file path and a diff of the changes.\n\n### 2. How to Approve Tool Calls\nWhen prompted, you have a few options:\n*   **Yes**: Approves only the current tool call.\n*   **Yes, don't ask again**: \n    *   **For Bash**: Creates a permanent `allow` rule for that specific command (or command prefix) within the current project directory.\n    *   **For File Edits**: Approves edits for the remainder of the current session.\n\n### 3. Permission Modes\nIf you find the repeated prompts disruptive, you can change the permission mode.\n\n**Quick Switch:** Press `Shift+Tab` to cycle through the available modes in the CLI:\n*   **Default**: Prompts for all file edits and shell commands.\n*   **Auto-accept edits (`acceptEdits`)**: Automatically allows file edits and common filesystem commands (like `mkdir` or `mv`), but still prompts for other Bash commands.\n*   **Plan mode (`plan`)**: Restricts Claude to read-only tools. It can analyze the code but cannot modify files or run commands.\n*   **Auto mode (`auto`)**: (Research Preview) Uses background safety checks to auto-approve actions that align with your request.\n*   **Bypass Permissions (`bypassPermissions`)**: Skips almost all prompts. **Warning**: Only use this in isolated environments (containers/VMs).\n\n**Persistent Config**: You can set your `defaultMode` in your `.claude/settings.json` file.\n\n### 4. Troubleshooting \"Permission Denied\" Without Prompts\nIf the agent reports a denial but you **do not see a prompt**, it means a `deny` rule is active. In Claude Code, permissions are evaluated in the order: **Deny $\\rightarrow$ Ask $\\rightarrow$ Allow**. A `deny` rule always takes precedence and blocks the action immediately without prompting.\n\n**Where to check for Deny rules:**\n1.  **Manage UI**: Run the `/permissions` command in the CLI to see a list of all active rules and where they are sourced from.\n2.  **Settings Files**: Check for a `permissions.deny` array in:\n    *   `.claude/settings.local.json` (Local project)\n    *   `.claude/settings.json` (Shared project)\n    *   `~/.claude/settings.json` (User global)\n3.  **Managed Settings**: If you are in a corporate environment, your administrator may have set managed policies that cannot be overridden.\n4.  **CLI Flags**: Check if the agent was started with the `--disallowedTools` flag.\n\nTo fix this, remove the offending rule from the `deny` list or move it to the `ask` or `allow` list using the `/permissions` UI."}],"model":"gemma-4-31b-it","stop_reason":"end_turn","stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":7252,"cache_creation_input_tokens":0,"cache_read_input_tokens":10210,"output_tokens":721,"server_tool_use":{"web_search_requests":0,"web_fetch_requests":0},"service_tier":"standard","cache_creation":{"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":0},"cache_deleted_input_tokens":0,"inference_geo":"","iterations":[],"speed":"standard"}},"requestId":"msg_083f94ca213b4775a2896e8513698f48","type":"assistant","uuid":"00cf1748-b615-4f4e-a493-bf35a19bb8ed","timestamp":"2026-05-05T07:54:25.585Z","userType":"external","entrypoint":"cli","cwd":"/Users/rayhanislamshuvro/Devloper/agent-openclaude","sessionId":"84bf0b5c-9d50-4cde-86ea-a96a4d44f2fb","version":"unknown","gitBranch":"HEAD","slug":"generic-wobbling-cake"}

## Document Structure

This map uses a hierarchical structure:

* **##** marks documentation groups (e.g., 'Getting started')
* **###** marks individual documentation pages
* **Nested bullets** show the heading structure within each page
* Each page title links to the full documentation

## Troubleshooting

### [troubleshoot-install](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/troubleshoot-install.md)

* Find your error
* Run diagnostic checks
  * Check network connectivity
  * Verify your PATH
  * Check for conflicting installations
  * Check directory permissions
  * Verify the binary works
* Common installation issues
  * Install script returns HTML instead of a shell script
  * `command not found: claude` after installation
  * `curl: (56) Failure writing output to destination`
  * TLS or SSL connection errors
  * `Failed to fetch version from downloads.claude.ai`
  * Wrong install command on Windows
  * `The process cannot access the file` during Windows install
  * Install killed on low-memory Linux servers
  * Install hangs in Docker
  * Claude Desktop overrides the `claude` command on Windows
  * Claude Code on Windows requires either Git for Windows (for bash) or PowerShell
  * Claude Code does not support 32-bit Windows
  * Linux musl or glibc binary mismatch
  * `Illegal instruction`
  * `dyld: cannot load` on macOS
  * `Exec format error` on WSL1
  * npm install errors in WSL
  * Permission errors during installation
  * Native binary not found after npm install
* Login and authentication
  * Reset your login
  * OAuth error: Invalid code
  * 403 Forbidden after login
  * This organization has been disabled with an active subscription
  * OAuth login fails in WSL2, SSH, or containers
  * Not logged in or token expired
  * Bedrock, Vertex, or Foundry credentials not loading
* Still stuck
